an OPTIONAL monthly/yearly subscription fee for the service/added cost to the one time purchase for a perpetual license

paragraphs.text: Dear Members of the European Commision! Im writting to highlight an issue many others already mentioned, which is Digital Products artificially beeing tied to online services (often unnecesarily) so in order to circumvent almost all currently existing Consumer Protection Laws. Which most notably video games are the prime (but not exclusive) most notable example of. Taking this into consideration this is a loophole which if not closed would undermine the current aims of the Act, since its only cover "products", NOT "services" and also feeds into the misleading/false marketing practices digital for digital products. Closing this loophole is paramouth for achieving the aims of the DFA and would also serve as an effectively address the issues of the "Stop Destroying Videogames" Citizens Initiative, which very likely gonna clear the 1 million signature in seven member nation tresshold. The most straightforward way to adress this loophole is for the Act to define the boundaries for "products" and "services" for digital goods, and enforce a clear separation between the two. For example: a one time purchase to use a software is a clear purchase for a "product" and hence the consumer should be entitled to perpetually use that particular version of the software; meanwhile the updates for that software is clearly a "service". This two segment in my opinion should be sold separately, a one time purchase for the product AND an OPTIONAL monthly/yearly subscription fee for the service/added cost to the one time purchase for a perpetual license which would entitle the consumer to software updates, online services etc. At least thats my suggestion of how to address the loophole and issue in question. Also, although its more closely related banking i think its worth highlighting, since its affecting consumers ability to even access digital products (and is a cause for uncertainty). In the past couple of years American payment processors have started to make legal purchases of legal products (both digital and physical) all but impossible purely for moral reasons. Normally it wouldnt be an issue, since if one company refuses the consumer have the option to use an another payment processor. The problem is Visa, Mastercard and Paypal all making these restrictions in the exact same time (which also raises some serious questions whether they are operating as a cartel or not), which leaves almost no avenue for consumers to purchase such arbitrarily restricted products. The issue became so absurd to the point, where the Japanese Fair Trade Commission already issued administrative sanctions against Visa for its practices of blocking payment to Japanese retailers, webshops etc. And i also like to point out to its also hindering the free trade within the EU itself in cases where both the retailer AND the consumer of these digital AND physical products are within the EU. Hence i would also urge the Commission to look into the posibility and feasability to prevent payment processors from completely blocking the purchase of legal goods and services in cases, where their refusal leaves no legal AND reasonable avenues to acquire said digital goods and services for the consumer with this Act.
